title: Recognize a Money Scam
summary: Spot warning signs of a financial scam and know how to respond.
learnerPromise: You will be able to recognize common signs of a financial scam and know what steps to take next.
whyItMatters: Scammers target people with urgent messages and fake offers; learning to spot the warning signs can protect your money and personal information from being stolen.
realLifeUses: Ignoring a text message claiming you won a prize you never entered
Hanging up on a caller pretending to be from your bank who asks for your password
Spotting a fake online seller asking for payment through unusual methods
Noticing a too-good-to-be-true investment offer and walking away
result: You can list the five most common scam warning signs and explain what to do if you encounter one.
youWillLearn: Identify pressure tactics like urgent deadlines or threats
Recognize requests for unusual payment methods like gift cards or wire transfers
Spot promises of guaranteed or risk-free returns
Know what to do and who to tell if you suspect a scam
miniChallenge: Read about one recent common scam online and identify which warning signs it uses.
steps: Learn the five common warning signs: urgency, secrecy, unusual payment methods, too-good-to-be-true offers, and requests for personal information.
Think of a recent message, call, or offer you received and check it against the warning signs.
If something feels off, pause and do not act immediately; scammers rely on rushed decisions.
Verify the claim independently by contacting the company or person directly through a known official channel.
If you suspect a scam, tell a trusted adult, contact your bank if money is involved, and report it to local authorities or a consumer protection agency.
completionCriteria: You can name at least five warning signs of a financial scam
You know who to contact if you or someone you know encounters a scam
commonProblems: Scammers using real company names and logos to look legitimate
Friends or family accounts being hacked to send scam messages
Feeling embarrassed after realizing a scam, which stops people from reporting it
tips: No legitimate company will ask for your password or PIN over the phone or by email
Gift cards are for gifts, not for paying bills or fines; any request to pay with gift cards is almost certainly a scam
If you are unsure, talk to a trusted adult, bank representative, or teacher before taking action
helpBoundary: If someone is pressuring you to send money quickly, or if you already shared financial information with a possible scammer, tell a trusted adult and contact your bank immediately. You are not in trouble; acting fast helps protect you.
